era_context: Mid-Hellenistic period. Antiochus IV Epiphanes rules the Seleucid Empire; Ptolemy VI Philometor rules Egypt under regency; Han Dynasty continues expansion in China. The Mediterranean world is entering a period of increasing conflict and Roman intervention.

events.title: Antiochus IV's early reign: Consolidation and expansion
events.description: Antiochus IV continues to consolidate his power and expand Seleucid influence. His reign is marked by military campaigns, administrative reforms, and cultural initiatives promoting Hellenization. These early years of his reign establish the patterns that will define his entire rule and eventually provoke resistance in Judea.
events.certainty_note: Antiochus IV's reign is well-attested, but specific events in 178 BCE are not clearly documented. The year falls within his early reign (175–164 BCE) and is assigned by convention.

events.title: Han Dynasty: Military campaigns and territorial consolidation
events.description: The Han Dynasty continues military operations to consolidate control over frontier regions and suppress Xiongnu resistance. These campaigns strengthen Han military capabilities and establish Chinese hegemony over Central Asian trade routes. The period marks the peak of Han military expansion under Emperor Xuan.
events.certainty_note: Han military campaigns during this period are attested in Chinese chronicles, but specific dating to 178 BCE is uncertain.

events.title: Ptolemaic Egypt: Regency and administrative stability
events.description: Cleopatra I continues as regent for Ptolemy VI. Egyptian administration remains stable, though the kingdom's international position is increasingly threatened by Seleucid expansion and Roman intervention. The regency period is characterized by careful diplomacy and internal consolidation.
events.certainty_note: Cleopatra I's regency is well-attested in papyri and Greek sources. Her death occurred around 176 BCE, so she was still regent in 178 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are securely dated to 178 BCE in surviving sources. The events listed are inferred from broader historical trends and assigned to this year by convention. It is possible that 178 BCE was not historically significant as a discrete year.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 178 BCE is sparse and derived entirely from later sources. No contemporary documentation exists for this specific year. The record is Mediterranean and East Asian-focused; other regions are absent. This year may be historically insignificant as a discrete point in time.

graph_edges.note: Part of Antiochus IV's broader reign
